real_questions: For each fund finance structure you use — rated note feeders, continuation vehicles, CFOs, NAV loans, subscription facilities — defend the LTV and advance-rate discipline.

real_questions.text: What minimum fixed charge coverage are you required to maintain under agreements with your parent, and what is your cushion against it?
What are dividend distributions as a percentage of net income, and what retention policy protects your liquidity buffer?
How confident are you in access to the unsecured markets — including continued access in more difficult funding environments?

In a stressed market, which of your funding sources are still there — and for off-take/forward-flow arrangements, what contracts are in place and will those buyers still be there if credit quality deteriorates?
Walk us through the encumbered versus unencumbered split of the asset base.
Your secured debt reliance is increasing — where does the funding mix go from here?
Do you have enough liquidity to support higher originations and meet debt maturities simultaneously?

common_pitfalls.text: Paying too much up to the parent — agencies flag high dividend payout ratios that leave the captive without retained earnings to support a liquidity buffer.

strong_answer_markers.text: Frames an ILC bank charter as funding diversification plus regulatory discipline — agencies view the charter, including its added regulation, as a credit positive.

agency_intel: Fund finance structural risks agencies raise, by structure: rated note feeders — LTVs, advance rates, portfolio concentration. Continuation vehicle financing — LTV, advance rates, concentration, volatile/risky underlying assets, cash sweep holidays. CFOs — volatile/risky underlying assets increasing. NAV loans — loan-to-value and advance rates are the biggest risk. Subscription facilities — tranching into subclasses is an increasing risk, along with LTVs. Issuers active in any of these should expect structure-specific questions.

agency_intel.text: Agencies view ILC bank charters for captives as a credit positive: low-cost funding access, further funding diversification, and the added regulation is read as increasing credit strength rather than as a burden.

Agencies are focused on declining fixed charge coverage at captives (know your covenant level and cushion) and on dividend distributions as a share of net income — the concern is over-distributing to the parent instead of retaining a liquidity buffer.

Agencies want a good amount of unsecured in a BDC's capital stack: it demonstrates funding diversification, shows creditors believe in the business, and leaves assets unencumbered — encumbrance pressures bondholders. Demonstrated public-market access is very helpful.

Funding diversification carries heavy weight for consumer lenders — warehouse + deposits + ABS + unsecured together is viewed very favorably. Realistic stress view: unsecured likely goes away in stress for lower credit quality; ABS gets stress-tested for resilience.
For leasing companies, more emphasis falls on encumbrance versus unencumbrance of assets, and less on asset quality — lead with the encumbered/unencumbered split.
This agency wants to see tremendous diversification in funding sources for consumer lenders — breadth across warehouse, deposits, ABS, and unsecured.
Funding and liquidity for CRE non-bank lenders has weakened modestly as secured debt reliance rises — agencies are watching the secured/unsecured trajectory closely.
